primary.justification: The chapter's entire architecture is organised around explaining and analysing the major historical change from mahājanapadas to the Mauryan Empire and its profound societal impact — politically (imperial governance, Aśhoka's transformation to dhamma), economically (trade, guilds, coins), culturally (spread of Buddhism, Brahmi script), and socially. Every named section — What is an Empire, Trade and Guilds, Rise of Magadha, Nanda Dynasty, Arrival of Greeks, The Mighty Mauryas, Aśhoka, Life in the Mauryan Period, Fragile Nature of Empires — addresses a dimension of this change or its societal impact. Remove this competency and the chapter's fundamental organising purpose dissolves.

Two architecturally distinct named sections develop this competency substantively: (1) 'Trade, Trade Routes and Guilds' is a dedicated section analysing how guilds (śhrenīs) organised the nature of work — craftsmen, traders, and agriculturists forming self-governing bodies with shared resources and skills; (2) 'Life in the Mauryan Period' is a dedicated section describing changes in habitation (Pāṭaliputra as planned urban centre), the nature of work (agriculture, artisans, blacksmiths, potters, carpenters, jewellers), and sociocultural achievements (art, architecture, edicts). Together these constitute substantive development of C-3.1 across two distinct named sections.

The Nanda dynasty's oppression and exploitation of people is described as a reason for its downfall, and the Aśhoka section traces the Kalinga war conflict and its transformation into a policy of non-violence and respect for all religious sects. These are named passages with conflict as a meaningful element within sections primarily about dynastic history and governance.

The Trade, Trade Routes and Guilds section explicitly analyses guilds as economic/social institutions. The Arthaśhāstra section lays out the saptānga — seven constituents of a kingdom as an institutional framework. These are named architectural elements focused on institutions, qualifying as present but not substantive.

The chapter highlights punch-marked coins as an Indian innovation, Kauṭilya's Arthaśhāstra as a foundational governance text, Aśhoka's international spread of Buddhist teachings, and Brahmi script as the mother of all regional Indian scripts — all framed as India's contributions. These appear as factual statements within historical narrative sections rather than a dedicated standalone section.
